messages.author: Brock
messages.text: I'm in Kamloops, so even bw 62.5 isn't going to cut it... We're messing around with paths to Vernon, Salmon Arm and Kelowna. Mt Lolo seems to have a good path that way. A casual test while working on VHF repeater gear confirmed it can be done, but a rain cell moved through and completely obliterated the path. 910 appears to be sensitive to that. 433 would cut through much better. As hams we can use it. Might try bridging 910 and 433 Heltec devices.

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: @ded (SalishMesh) thanks for that. Our group up here are mostly radio guys, so we're more interested in coverage and reliability than the underlying firmware. And not even that interested in chatting with it either, as far as that goes! The biggest challenge I have with it is understanding what the spectrum looks like without an S meter or waterfall display. SNR is great, but it's rarely consistent, so inferring what's happening is not simple. I will take my TinySA Ultra up the mountain next time and see what it can tell me. Given the standard settings are what the new users will use, getting the word out about non-default settings is the challenge. I've also wondered about adjusting the frequency a bit too... But need to investigate thoroughly with the SA first.

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: It will be interesting to see what more elevation and gain might do over the longer hops, but I suspect that the limitation will be the frequency and realistic power output. 433 is where the long jumps need to be. I believe the firmware supports bridging on some devices - the Heltec V3 being one. Unfortunately 433 is Ham. But there are devices that operate there (like garage door openers) that seem to be ignored by ISED. I'm not suggesting that regulations be broken, but it sure would be interesting to compare performance...
messages.content_html: It will be interesting to see what more elevation and gain might do over the longer hops, but I suspect that the limitation will be the frequency and realistic power output. 433 is where the long jumps need to be. I believe the firmware supports bridging on some devices - the Heltec V3 being one. Unfortunately 433 is Ham. But there are devices that operate there (like garage door openers) that seem to be ignored by ISED. I'm not suggesting that regulations be broken, but it sure would be interesting to compare performance...

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: Anyone who might be interested...we activated a new repeater on Mt Lolo on Monday. That was the missing link to connect through to Vernon and further south into the Okanagan. It doesn't seem like the links are super reliable yet though. And yes, we are running the 62.5 BW etc. It really seems like the repeaters stop adverting after a while. Either that or the app isn't decoding the traffic properly. For example, the Mt Lolo repeater hasn't been heard adverting since the first...but I'm hearing other repeater adverts that would be impossible if Lolo wasn't repeating. I'm not sure what to think about this.
messages.content_html: Anyone who might be interested...we activated a new repeater on Mt Lolo on Monday. That was the missing link to connect through to Vernon and further south into the Okanagan. It doesn't seem like the links are super reliable yet though. And yes, we are running the 62.5 BW etc. It really seems like the repeaters stop adverting after a while. Either that or the app isn't decoding the traffic properly. For example, the Mt Lolo repeater hasn't been heard adverting since the first...but I'm hearing other repeater adverts that would be impossible if Lolo wasn't repeating. I'm not sure what to think about this.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: Yup, I'm aware of the time sync requirement. I suppose it's possible that there was a power blip on the mountain that rebooted the repeater, but based on other equipment on the same power source, I don't think that happened. Lolo is very strong into town, so the fact that adverts it repeats from a hop or two beyond are heard regularly, but Lolo is not, seems suspect.

messages.author: ka.RFz
messages.text: I'd like to experiment with a few different antennas on these repeaters. VE7UBC North, VE7NWR New West and the KA RAK4631 test repeater all use the same or similar 8dBi omni. From looking at the packet analyzer over the past few days, the receive sensitivity doesn't seem nearly as good as it should be when comparing to other repeaters down south. The vertical beamwidth on the 8dBi units are pretty narrow so that is may be playing into it. Would be good to get a run down of what antennas you all are using at your high/medium elevation sites.

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: We've got a pretty solid link between Kamloops and Vernon now. And beyond, but nobody in Kelowna or Salmon Arm seems to be active yet. Four repeaters involved. Adding all the distances between them would sum to over 100 km, between mountaintops. Some behavior I've noticed and wonder if the Salish hops see similar...: I often see 'delivered' appear very shortly after the second attempt begins. I'm wondering if the second attempt is made before the ack from the first one can get back to me? Similarly, red failures sometimes flip to green delivered. Is my guess reasonable? Does the code look at the route and determine a realistic delay to use before a retry is sent?

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: Lolo is a good site. Quiet. Good line of sight to several other sites. Dufferin was a lost cause. We've set different TX delays on each repeater. And went with a really short AGC reset value, as most of them have MT built into the same unit too. I suspect both tweaks were important.
messages.content_html: Lolo is a good site. Quiet. Good line of sight to several other sites. Dufferin was a lost cause. We've set different TX delays on each repeater. And went with a really short AGC reset value, as most of them have MT built into the same unit too. I suspect both tweaks were important.

messages.author: Brock
messages.text: The thing about gain is you can't magically increase the effective power without giving something up. A Yagi's dB rating is based on concentrating the radiated power in one direction, so it has a dB rating above that of the theoretical isotropic radiator. In the case of an omnidirectional antenna with a higher gain, that typically means that the signal is more concentrated horizontally, which means receivers higher and lower than the plane of signal will suffer. So if you want to pursue gain numbers, you need to recognize your compromise. There's no magic and there's no free lunch, unfortunately.
messages.content_html: The thing about gain is you can't magically increase the effective power without giving something up. A Yagi's dB rating is based on concentrating the radiated power in one direction, so it has a dB rating above that of the theoretical isotropic radiator. In the case of an omnidirectional antenna with a higher gain, that typically means that the signal is more concentrated horizontally, which means receivers higher and lower than the plane of signal will suffer. So if you want to pursue gain numbers, you need to recognize your compromise. There's no magic and there's no free lunch, unfortunately.

messages.author: Mistial Dev
messages.text: Background: At its core, we have a geographic area that's vulnerable due to sociopolitical considerations and geographic considerations. One road in and out, through the border agencies. One ISP, one power company. When the lines on the way in go down, we can lose comms and power. The tower is aging, we control one half and pay for repairs, though the county may take over that at some point. We have the PREP group, which is the prepper group. Many are HAMs. They are focused on taking care of themselves and their neighbors. Free spirits, not under any command or control. We have something like 50 members, 80% of which are Canadians (under training visas, I believe, excluding dual citizens/dual residents). We have very few fires (fortunately!), so we are doing lots of training. We have CERT (Community Emergency Response Team), which is under the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) chain of command, and affiliated with the county. We can activate them. They have training to shut off gas lines, tag people for mass casualties, etc. They will be activated when we need bodies, as was the case with Search and Rescue. Right now, communications is through WhatsAPP. The local agency members have pagers that do two tone. That's the UHF repeater link. We're the front line. CERT is the additional manpower. The department has 3 legs - Emergency Medicine, Search and Rescue, and Fire. PREP does ... things outside those scopes. A bit more independent. We had a SAR activation of CERT, and ran into difficulties getting the word out quickly and effectively at night to people without dedicated pagers, due to silenced phones, people sleeping, muted notifications in the new IOS AI thing, etc. This limited the amount of manpower that could be brought to bear until people woke up.

messages.text: Near Christmas Church Cathedral from inside my place at ground level, on Dec 24 and 25, I was suddenly able to receive hundreds of packets with first hop being 86 and 42. I just happened to luck out when I put it where I did that night, hit the sweet spot. I haven't received anything at home since that time from either router. I am not sure what 42 is, think it might be what has been named Repeater 4201 and Infinite Improbability.

messages.author: MrAlders0n
messages.text: Popping in from MeshMapper. I understand TX wardriving isn't ideal for mesh load. We've built in safeguards to address this. Each region gets an admin panel with a designated POC who can limit TX wardriving slots. Even disable and stop all active wardriving if they deem required. We felt like we had to build in this admin function as we were worried about Mesh load. When users attempt to connect and no slots are available, they automatically fall back to passive wardriving only. Slots free up as other users disconnect. This approach prevents our app from overwhelming the mesh with transmit activity while still enabling active data collection when capacity allows. (edited)
messages.content_html: Popping in from MeshMapper. I understand TX wardriving isn't ideal for mesh load. We've built in safeguards to address this. Each region gets an admin panel with a designated POC who can limit TX wardriving slots. Even disable and stop all active wardriving if they deem required. We felt like we had to build in this admin function as we were worried about Mesh load. When users attempt to connect and no slots are available, they automatically fall back to passive wardriving only. Slots free up as other users disconnect. messages.author: d.blair
messages.text: Govt are inherently risk adverse. I approached one 2 years ago. Their issues we're; hosting could be seen as endorsing a network that they have no control over how it's used, they don't have direct benifits to participating in, and could interfere with existing emergency comms. It was a hard sell and ultimately a "no". But I'd encourage trying!

messages.author: Just Stuff™
messages.text: Good morning fellow meshengers I've been working on a free new tool to visualize actual repeater coverage using the node discover feature. It draws color coded hexagonal layers on a map feel free to use it at will. All that is required for initial setup is a meshcore ble companion radio. It runs in your browser online or offline with optional map caching. What Is Mesh Utility? Mesh Utility helps you see how well your LoRa mesh radio network covers an area. Walk or drive around with your radio connected, and the app builds a color-coded map showing where your signal is strong, weak, or missing entirely. On iPhones and iPads, you'll need the Bluefy or WebBLE browser since Safari doesn't support this type of Bluetooth. On Android, computers, or Chromebooks, use Chrome or Edge. Once connected, the app shows your radio's name, battery level, and frequency at the top of the settings panel. The screen will stay awake while connected so you don't lose your Bluetooth link during scans. If you put the app in the background (especially on iOS), the Bluetooth connection may drop. When you return, the app checks automatically and lets you know if you need to reconnect. The app sends out a discovery signal and listens for any repeaters that respond, measuring how strong and clear each signal is. Each scan cycle takes about 20 seconds. Your GPS location (and altitude when available) is recorded with each scan so the map knows exactly where you were. Green means excellent signal. Yellow-green is good. Yellow is fair. Orange is marginal. Red is poor. Dark red means no repeater responded at all (a dead zone). Purple means the signal power is okay but clarity is bad, usually from interference. Tap any hex to see its details: signal readings, how many scans were taken there, altitude, and which repeaters were heard. https://mesh-utility.replit.app/manual
